title: Installing a vapour control layer
subtitle: When to fit a VCL, which side of the insulation it goes, and how to seal it
excerpt: A vapour control layer limits moisture-laden indoor air from reaching the cold face of insulation where it would condense. It goes on the warm side of the insulation, every joint and penetration must be taped airtight, and it should never be fitted in a breathable-insulation system or behind a lime-plastered historic wall.
sourceNotes: Gov.uk Building Regulations Part C (site preparation and resistance to contaminants and moisture). BRE Good Repair Guide 9 on condensation in buildings. AECB Warm House Retrofit Guide on vapour control strategies. BS EN 13984:2013 for VCL product performance classes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A membrane installed on the warm side of insulation to resist vapour diffusion and prevent interstitial condensation within the insulation or the wall structure.
Glass or rock fibre insulation. Unlike closed-cell foam boards, mineral wool is vapour-open and normally requires a VCL on the warm side when used in walls and roofs.

content.text: (VCL) is a plastic or foil-faced membrane that slows the movement of water vapour through a wall, floor, or roof from the warm inside to the cold outside. Without it, warm humid air migrates into the insulation, cools, and deposits moisture: a process called interstitial condensation. Over seasons this wets
and reduces its insulating performance. In timber-frame construction it also rots the frame.
Between internal wall insulation (mineral wool between battens) and the plasterboard room face. This applies in modern houses with impermeable masonry or concrete block walls.
In a cold-side loft: insulation at rafter level makes the loft cold. A VCL at the warm ceiling face stops moisture rising into the rafter space.
In suspended floor insulation where the subfloor void is not well ventilated.
When a VCL must NOT be used
Behind breathable insulation on a historic lime-mortar wall. The VCL would trap moisture from the masonry behind it, raising wall moisture content and causing decay at embedded timbers.

With closed-cell foam boards (PIR or XPS) that have their own integral vapour control provided by the foil or plastic facing. Adding a second VCL creates a double barrier that can trap moisture between the two membranes.
Always on the warm side. In a UK climate this means on the inside face of the insulation (between insulation and plasterboard). Never on the cold side.
Continuous sheet with overlapping joints. Overlap sheet edges by at least 150 mm. Tape every joint with vapour-control tape (not standard duct tape, which fails at temperature extremes).
Seal every penetration. A pipe or cable passing through the VCL is a bypass. Use proprietary pipe-through grommets or a generous fold of VCL taped to the pipe with compression clamps.

Extend the VCL past the insulation edge to the structural element at the perimeter. At a wall-ceiling junction, tape the VCL sheet from the wall insulation up to the ceiling VCL in a continuous folded lap. Gaps at junctions are where most VCL systems fail in practice.

Products are classified by their vapour resistance (sd value, in metres equivalent air layer). A standard polythene VCL has sd of 50-100 m. A smart VCL (vapour-variable membrane) has sd of 1-10 m in summer (allowing the wall to dry inward) and 30-100 m in winter (preventing winter condensation). Smart VCLs are worth using in high-humidity spaces (bathrooms, kitchen walls) where the balance of drying and limiting matters most.
